title: Rosemary analgesic bath
subtitle: A warming bath for muscular aching and stiffness
excerpt: A concentrated rosemary infusion strained and added to a warm bath. Traditionally used for muscular aching, poor circulation in the limbs, and general stiffness after exertion.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer,, A Modern Herbal, rosemary entry: the circulatory, analgesic bath, and tonic tradition. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, rosemary entry, the external bath use. the herbal reference on Rosmarini folium, traditional use for supportive therapy of minor muscle aches.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rosemary concentrate can be made 2-3 hours ahead and stored cool until needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that promotes blood flow, particularly to peripheral tissues. Rosemary's volatile oil is a circulatory stimulant; the bath preparation delivers it through skin absorption and inhalation of the steam.

content.text: A rosemary bath is one of the most accessible

preparations in the western herbal tradition. Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer, records rosemary baths for rheumatic pains and muscular aching. The aromatic oil is absorbed through the skin and inhaled from the steam. Traditional western herbal use covers minor muscle aches. The preparation is a concentrated infusion of dried rosemary (100 g in 1 litre of water) strained and added to a warm bath. The result is aromatic, warming, and noticeably stimulating to tired muscles.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy. A warm bath with a culinary sprig of rosemary added is standard and safe. A concentrated rosemary bath (100 g dried herb) is outside the culinary dose range; consult your midwife before therapeutic bath preparations in pregnancy.

Cardiovascular conditions. A very warm bath is itself a circulatory challenge; readers with significant heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, or circulatory conditions should use a comfortably warm (not hot) bath temperature and limit bath time to 15 minutes.

Bath safety. Never take a bath that is hot enough to cause dizziness or faintness. Get out of the bath carefully; the circulatory stimulation can cause brief orthostatic dizziness when standing.

items.prepNote: dried rosemary; or 200 g fresh rosemary sprigs, roughly bruised
just off the boil for the concentrate

content.text: Prepare the concentrate: put the dried rosemary in a large saucepan. Pour over the hot water. Cover and steep for 20 minutes. Strain through a sieve, pressing the herb firmly. You should have about 800-900 ml of very dark, strongly aromatic liquid. Run the bath to a comfortable warm temperature (37-39°C; not hot enough to be uncomfortable). Add the strained rosemary concentrate to the bath water just before getting in. Soak for 15-20 minutes, breathing in the aromatic steam.

One bath treatment, once daily if needed. Soak for 15-20 minutes. Prepare as needed during an episode of muscular aching; there is no fixed course length. Rest for 30 minutes after the bath; the circulatory stimulation means you will feel warm and slightly tired afterward.
When not to use this remedy

Not for readers with heart disease or high blood pressure without medical guidance. Avoid at therapeutic dose in pregnancy. Any aching following injury that might be a muscle tear or fracture needs medical assessment first.
